01.rationale: Teaching higher education pedagogy involves significant digital work products (lecture materials, course content, assessments) that AI can help generate or enhance, and many tasks like content summarization, feedback generation, and curriculum design are increasingly automatable. However, the core work requires substantial human judgment, interpersonal interaction, mentoring, and live instruction that cannot be fully replaced by current AI, preventing a higher score. The occupation is moderately-to-highly exposed to AI augmentation rather than replacement.

011.rationale: Docenten in art history at higher education have moderately-to-high AI exposure. While teaching requires interpersonal interaction, mentoring, and nuanced judgment that AI cannot fully replace, significant portions of their work—course preparation, content creation, research synthesis, curriculum development, and administrative tasks—are increasingly automatable or augmentable by AI tools (LLMs for lecture drafting, content summarization; computer vision for art analysis). However, the creative, evaluative, and human-centered aspects of teaching and the need for real-time classroom presence limit complete automation.

However, core work requires significant hands-on skills (soldering, component alignment, physical testing, troubleshooting) that demand manual dexterity, on-site presence, and real-world sensory judgment that current AI cannot fully replace.

As AI systems become more sophisticated in understanding complex distributed systems and security implications, this occupation faces very high exposure to automation and AI-augmented workflows.

072.rationale: Photography teaching involves a mix of automatable and non-automatable tasks. The hands-on, experiential nature of photography education and the need to guide individual artistic growth limits AI's ability to replace this occupation.

0721.rationale: This occupation has high AI exposure due to its significant digital and analytical components: CAD software usage, technical drawing interpretation, data analysis for material calculations, project progress tracking, and compliance monitoring can all be substantially automated or enhanced by AI tools. However, the role retains non-automatable elements including on-site inspection of materials, coordination with architects and stakeholders, safety oversight, and judgment calls on complex engineering problems that require contextual understanding and real-world site presence.

While routine monitoring, data analysis, quality control documentation, and process optimization tasks are increasingly automatable through AI and sensor systems, the role requires on-site physical presence, real-time decision-making in complex industrial environments, safety oversight, and management of human workers—tasks that remain difficult for current AI to fully replace. The work combines digital/analytical components (vulnerable to automation) with critical supervisory and interpersonal responsibilities (requiring human judgment).

0821.rationale: ICT-helpdeskmanagers work primarily with digital systems, ticketing platforms, and remote support tools, making their work largely automatable through AI-powered chatbots, automated troubleshooting, and ticket routing systems. 0921.rationale: Agricultural machinery operators require significant physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time sensory judgment to operate heavy equipment safely in variable outdoor conditions. 1011.rationale: Hospital pharmacists work with primarily digital systems (pharmacy databases, electronic health records, drug interaction software) and perform routine tasks like medication dispensing, compounding, and monitoring that are increasingly automatable through robotics and AI. 1012.rationale: Specialized nurses perform a mix of automatable and non-automatable tasks. 1013.rationale: Fysiotherapy requires extensive in-person physical interaction, manual dexterity, and real-time assessment of patient responses that cannot be fully automated by current AI. 102.rationale: Social work requires significant in-person interaction, emotional intelligence, and real-world presence that cannot be fully replaced by AI. 1041.rationale: Social workers require significant in-person presence, emotional intelligence, and judgment-based decision-making that AI cannot replicate. The work products are not digital, and the occupation fundamentally requires physical presence and sensory judgment.

1222.rationale: This occupation is highly physical and manual, requiring constant real-world presence to collect waste from homes and facilities. The core tasks—lifting, emptying bins, and operating collection equipment—demand hands-on dexterity and cannot be performed remotely. While documentation of waste collection could theoretically be digitized, it remains a minor supplementary task to work that is fundamentally tied to physical labor.

13.rationale: Groupage-expediteurs work with highly digital processes including document management (bills of lading, export permits), tariff analysis, rate publishing, and cargo coordination systems. Most core tasks involve data processing, documentation, and administrative work that can be automated or significantly augmented by AI tools. However, the role requires negotiation skills, client relationship management, and understanding of maritime regulations that still demand human judgment and communication.

131.rationale: Groupage-expediteurs work primarily with digital documentation (bills of lading, manifests, customs permits, tariff analysis) and logistics coordination that can be increasingly automated by AI systems for document processing, scheduling, and data analysis. However, the role requires significant human judgment in negotiating with clients, managing complex international regulations, and coordinating physical shipping operations that still depend on real-world expertise and relationship management.

1311.rationale: Kraamverzorgenden (postpartum care nurses) perform highly physical, hands-on work requiring constant real-world presence: assisting with deliveries, monitoring vital signs, providing direct patient care, and handling emergency situations. This occupation demands sensory judgment, physical touch, emotional presence, and immediate responsiveness to patients that cannot be automated or performed remotely by current or near-future AI technology.
